AI that knows your world

RAG, Vector
& Graph Databases

How modern AI finds, connects, and explains your world — with answers you can verify.

"Finds" refers to retrieval-augmented generation: AI that searches a document library at the moment of answering, rather than relying only on what it memorised during training. "Connects" refers to graph databases: storing knowledge as a network of entities and relationships so that multi-step reasoning is possible. "Explains" refers to the end result: answers that point to the source document rather than appearing from nowhere. All three topics address the same underlying problem — that raw AI models are powerful reasoners but poor knowledge holders. They hallucinate when they don't know something, they go stale as the world changes, and they cannot justify their answers. The three technologies in this deck are the standard response to those three problems.
“
An AI that can’t look things up is like a doctor who stopped reading after medical school — confident, but out of date.
The case for retrieval-augmented AI
A doctor trained in 2005 and never exposed to new research retains real expertise and the ability to reason — but medicine advances. New drugs, new diagnostic criteria, new treatment protocols. That doctor would answer confidently using outdated information, and the patient would have no way to know the difference. AI models work the same way: their knowledge crystallises at training time, and from that point forward every new development is invisible to them. Unlike a doctor who might say "I'm not sure, let me check," AI models are optimised to produce fluent, confident-sounding responses — so they fill knowledge gaps with invented content that reads as authoritative. "Confident, but out of date" captures both failure modes at once: the confidence is real, and so is the staleness.

The challenge

AI knows a lot — but not everything

Frozen

Knowledge locked at training time — the AI doesn’t know what happened last week

Hallucinations

Confident answers that are simply wrong — the model fills gaps with plausible guesses

Black boxes

No way to trace where an answer came from or why the AI said it

This is why standard AI fails in business settings

Medical, legal, and financial teams need answers they can audit and verify — not plausible-sounding guesses. The rest of this deck shows how to fix all three problems.

"Frozen" — AI models are trained on a fixed dataset with a hard cutoff date. Everything after that date is invisible: updated regulations, new products, recent events, revised policies. The model cannot absorb new information after deployment without being fully retrained — an expensive process that takes weeks and significant compute. "Hallucinations" — language models are trained to predict the most statistically plausible next word. When they encounter a question whose answer falls outside their training data, they still generate a response — the most plausible-sounding one. That response may be entirely fabricated: invented names, invented statistics, invented citations. The model expresses no uncertainty because it has no mechanism to distinguish "I know this" from "I'm guessing this." "Black boxes" — even when an AI gives a correct answer, it cannot point to which source it drew from. There is no document to open, no page to cite, no passage to verify. In regulated industries — healthcare, legal, financial — this lack of auditability makes AI answers legally unusable, no matter how accurate they appear. The three problems compound: a frozen knowledge base increases the likelihood of hallucinations on recent topics, and the black-box nature means neither the user nor the system can identify which answers are affected.
01

Act 1

How AI looks things up

"Looks things up" distinguishes retrieval-augmented AI from standard AI in a single phrase. A standard AI answers from memory. A retrieval-augmented AI queries an external document library at the moment the question arrives, reads the relevant passages, and uses those as the basis for its answer. Looking things up requires understanding what is being asked (not just matching keywords), finding the right documents in milliseconds, and synthesising across multiple sources — all without the user knowing anything about the underlying process. The technical term is retrieval-augmented generation: retrieval (the search step) augmenting generation (the answer-writing step).

The core idea

Closed book vs open book AI

Closed book

Standard AI

Knows only what it learned during training. Once deployed, its knowledge is frozen.

  • Cannot learn from new documents
  • Answers are hard to verify
  • No citations or sources
  • Makes things up when unsure

Open book

AI with retrieval

Looks up relevant information at the moment of answering — just like checking a reference book.

  • Reads your latest documents
  • Every answer has a traceable source
  • Can cite specific passages
  • Knowledge stays up to date
Standard AI compresses everything it learned during training into internal numeric weights — millions of parameters encoding statistical patterns across billions of words. Those weights are fixed after training. "Cannot learn from new documents" means literally that: you cannot hand the model a PDF the way you hand a file to a colleague. "Answers are hard to verify" follows directly — the model's source is itself, not a document that can be opened and checked. "No citations" is the same problem stated differently. "Makes things up when unsure" is the hallucination failure mode: the model generates the most statistically plausible response regardless of whether it corresponds to fact. The right side reverses all four. "Reads your latest documents" — the document library is a separate system that can be updated at any time, completely independently of the AI model. "Every answer has a traceable source" — the passages that informed the answer are part of the system's output, not hidden inside weights. "Can cite specific passages" — not just the document name but the exact excerpt used. "Knowledge stays up to date" — updating a policy document or adding a new product specification immediately changes what the AI can correctly answer, with no retraining.

Step by step

A question becomes an answer in 4 steps

1 · Ask

A user types a question — “What is our refund policy for enterprise customers?”

2 · Search

The system searches your document library for the most relevant passages — matching meaning, not just keywords.

3 · Read

The best-matching passages are collected and handed to the AI — these become its “open book.”

4 · Answer

The AI writes a grounded response based on the retrieved passages — and can cite the exact source it used.

Step 1 "Ask" — the question arrives in natural language. Before search begins, the question is converted into a mathematical representation of its meaning — a list of numbers encoding what the question is about, not just what words appear in it. Step 2 "Search" — that numerical representation is compared against the stored representations of every document passage in the library. The comparison is a distance calculation: passages whose meaning is close to the question's meaning score high. The system can scan millions of passages in under a second and return a ranked list of the most relevant ones. This search understands "car crash" and "automobile collision" as near-identical queries, even though they share no words. Step 3 "Read" — the top-ranked passages (typically 5–20) are extracted and assembled into a context block. This is the material the AI will use to compose its answer. It functions as a temporary, question-specific briefing document. Step 4 "Answer" — the AI reads the assembled passages and generates a response grounded in what it found. It can quote specific sentences, reason across multiple passages, and identify when the retrieved material doesn't contain a sufficient answer — in which case it can say so rather than invent.

Before the question arrives

Turning documents into searchable meaning

CHUNK
📄
Document
raw text
split \u2192
✂
Chunk
passage-sized pieces
embed \u2192
🧭
Embed
meaning vector
store \u2192
🗂
Index
ready for query time
This is the work that happens before a user ever types a question \u2014 the ingestion pipeline. "Chunk" \u2014 a long document is split into passage-sized pieces, typically a few hundred words each, small enough that a single chunk covers one coherent idea and large enough to preserve context. Chunk size is a real design decision: too small and a chunk loses surrounding context; too large and irrelevant material dilutes the embedding. "Embed" \u2014 each chunk is passed through an embedding model that outputs a vector: a list of numbers encoding what the chunk means. Two chunks about the same topic produce vectors that land close together in this number space, regardless of the exact wording. "Index" \u2014 the vector, plus the original text and any metadata (source document, page, date), is stored in the vector database's index structure so it can be found quickly later. This whole pipeline runs once per document, ahead of time \u2014 it is completely separate from the 4-step query loop shown earlier, which runs once per question. Slow, thorough indexing up front buys fast, cheap search at query time.

The impact

Three things this makes possible

Accurate

Answers grounded in real documents — not invented from statistical patterns

Fresh

Connect to live or regularly updated sources — no expensive retraining needed

Traceable

Every answer points to its source — auditable, compliant, and trustworthy

Why enterprise AI is built on retrieval

Bigger models improve fluency — but retrieval improves trust. The most valuable AI systems combine both.

"Accurate" — retrieval constrains the AI to what is actually in the document library. If the answer is present, the AI finds and uses it. If it isn't, the system can report that no relevant document was found rather than inventing one. Accuracy is not guaranteed — the retrieved passages must themselves be correct, and the AI must interpret them correctly — but the hallucination failure mode (inventing plausible-sounding facts from nothing) is largely eliminated. "Fresh" — the document library is entirely separate from the AI model. Adding a new policy document, a product update, or a regulatory change to the library immediately makes that information available to the AI, with no change to the model and no retraining. The library can be updated on any schedule — daily imports, real-time feeds, or manual uploads. "Traceable" — every answer carries a provenance chain: which passages were retrieved, which documents they came from, and which sections of the source material the AI drew from. This makes it possible to audit any answer after the fact, verify it against the original source, and identify exactly where a wrong answer went wrong. In healthcare, legal, and financial contexts, this traceability is often a regulatory requirement, not a preference. The why panel captures the broader shift: fluency and reasoning are baseline expectations for modern AI — most large models have those. The differentiator in production is trust. Retrieval is the mechanism that converts capable AI into trustworthy AI.
02

Act 2

Searching by meaning

"Searching by meaning" is the defining capability of vector databases. A standard database can search by exact value: find all records where the city field equals "London." A keyword search engine can search by word occurrence: find all documents containing the word "affordable." Neither can answer: find all documents that are about the concept of finding a low-cost place to stay in London — even if they use entirely different words. That is what meaning search does. It represents both the query and the documents as mathematical objects, and finds the documents whose mathematical representation is closest to the query's. Vector databases are the storage and query layer that makes this possible at the scale of millions of documents in milliseconds.

How search evolved

Words, then meaning, then both

Era 1 — 1990s

Keyword search

Matches exact words. Great for precise terms. Misses synonyms, paraphrases, or related concepts.

Google 1998 · Ctrl+F

Era 2 — AI-powered

Meaning search

Understands what you’re asking, not just the words used. “Heart attack” matches “myocardial infarction.” Powered by neural AI.

Vector databases

Era 3 — Today

Combined search

Uses both precision and meaning. Best of exact matching and semantic understanding for production AI.

Modern AI systems

Era 1 — Keyword search builds an index of every word in every document. When a query arrives, it looks up which documents contain those words and ranks them by how frequently and prominently the words appear. This is fast and precise for exact terminology, but completely blind to paraphrase. A search for "heart failure" returns nothing if the document discusses "cardiac arrest" — clinically related, lexically unrelated. Era 2 — Meaning search converts text into a dense numerical vector: a list of hundreds or thousands of numbers that encodes the semantic content of the text. Two pieces of text that mean the same thing produce vectors that are close together in this number space, even if they share no words. This capability existed in research form for decades but became dramatically more capable with modern neural language models, which can represent nuanced meaning across long passages — not just individual word similarities. Vector databases provide the infrastructure to store millions of these representations and retrieve the closest matches to any new query. Era 3 — The two approaches are complementary rather than competing. Keyword search handles exact matches that meaning search can miss: product codes, legal clause numbers, proper names, precise identifiers. Meaning search handles natural language and paraphrase that keyword search cannot reach. Production AI systems combine both: keyword search for precision, meaning search for understanding.

Vector databases

Storing meaning, not just words

A vector database converts words, sentences, and documents into coordinates in space. Documents with similar meanings end up close together — regardless of the exact words used.

  • Finds the right answer even with different phrasing
  • Works across languages and content types
  • Scales to millions of documents in milliseconds
  • Powers image search, voice AI, and chatbots

Real-world example

Spotify’s music recommendations

Spotify doesn’t match songs by title or artist. It converts each song into a fingerprint based on tempo, key, energy, and texture.

Songs that feel similar are stored near each other in this fingerprint space. When you like a song, Spotify finds the nearest songs and recommends those.

Vector databases do exactly this for text, images, and more. Instead of audio fingerprints, they use meaning fingerprints.

"Coordinates in space" — when a document is added to a vector database, a neural model reads it and outputs a list of numbers, typically hundreds to thousands of values, collectively representing the meaning of that text. This is called an embedding vector. Two documents about the same topic produce similar vectors; two documents about unrelated topics produce distant ones. Storing this vector alongside the document means the database can later find documents whose vectors are closest to a query vector — in other words, documents whose meaning is most similar to the query's meaning. "Finds the right answer even with different phrasing" follows from this directly. "Works across languages" is possible because the models can be trained to produce similar vectors for translations of the same text. "Scales to millions" because comparing lists of numbers is a fast mathematical operation, even across a very large index. The Spotify example on the right applies the same architecture to music. Each song is converted into a fingerprint — a list of numbers encoding tempo, key, energy, acousticness, and dozens of audio features. Songs with similar fingerprints are stored near each other in this space. When a user plays a track, Spotify finds the tracks with the nearest fingerprints and recommends those — without knowing the genre, artist, or title. The algorithm sees only the numbers. Vector databases for text work identically, replacing audio features with semantic features extracted from the content.

Vector jargon, decoded

Four words worth knowing

Every document becomes an \u2014 a coordinate in meaning-space. Two embeddings are compared with , a score for how closely their directions align. Scanning millions of embeddings one by one would be too slow, so vector databases build an graph \u2014 one popular index structure \u2014 to jump straight to the closest neighbors instead of checking every candidate.

Click any underlined term for its full definition popup \u2014 this slide exists so the jargon in the rest of the deck (and in any vector-database documentation the audience reads afterward) has a fixed reference point. "Embedding" is the output of the embed step from the previous slide: a dense numeric vector representing meaning. "Cosine similarity" is the most common way to compare two embeddings \u2014 it measures the angle between them rather than their raw magnitude, which matters because embedding length can vary with text length while the direction (the meaning) is what should be compared. "HNSW" (Hierarchical Navigable Small World) is the specific graph-based index structure most production vector databases (Qdrant, Weaviate, Milvus) use under the hood \u2014 it organizes vectors into layered graphs so a search can skip most of the index and still find the true nearest neighbors with high probability. "ANN" (Approximate Nearest Neighbor) is the broader category HNSW belongs to: an algorithm that trades a small, tunable amount of accuracy for a large speedup over checking every vector exhaustively \u2014 the trade that makes millisecond search over millions of documents possible at all.

Hands-on

What retrieval looks like in code

Five lines, one answer

Embedding a query and searching a vector index is a handful of calls against a client library \u2014 no custom math required.

  • Embed Turn the question into a vector
  • Search Ask the index for nearest neighbors
  • Use Feed the top passages to the LLM
# retrieve.py
from qdrant_client import QdrantClient

client = QdrantClient("localhost", port=6333)
query_vector = embed("refund policy for enterprise?")

# nearest-neighbor search over the index
hits = client.search(
    collection_name="docs",
    query_vector=query_vector,
    limit=5,
)
This slide grounds everything so far in real, runnable code. `embed(...)` stands in for a call to an embedding model (OpenAI, Cohere, or a local sentence-transformer) that converts the query string into the same kind of vector every document chunk was converted into during ingestion. `client.search(...)` is the actual approximate-nearest-neighbor lookup discussed on the jargon slide \u2014 the client library hides the HNSW graph traversal entirely; the caller just gets back `limit` results ordered by similarity. The reason this is worth showing in code, not just in a diagram, is to make the abstraction concrete: "vector search" is not a mysterious black box, it is a network call to a database with a query vector as the argument, functionally similar to a SQL `SELECT ... ORDER BY similarity LIMIT 5`. Engineers already comfortable with database clients can map this pattern onto a vector database in minutes.

Hands-on

The same search, from a terminal

zsh \u2014 vector-db 
$ curl -s localhost:6333/collections/docs/points/search \
    -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    -d '{"vector": [0.12, -0.03, ...], "limit": 5}'
# querying HNSW index over 480,000 points\u2026
\u2713 5 nearest neighbors in 8ms
\u2713 top score: 0.91 (cosine)
Same operation as the previous slide's Python client, called directly against the vector database's HTTP API with `curl` \u2014 useful for debugging, health checks, or any language without a dedicated client library. The request body is the query embedding (truncated here for space) plus how many results to return. The two numbers in the output are worth calling out: "8ms" is the latency of the ANN search itself \u2014 not the embedding step, not the LLM generation, just the index lookup \u2014 across nearly half a million stored points, which is the entire value proposition of approximate nearest-neighbor search over exhaustive comparison. "0.91 (cosine)" is the similarity score of the best match \u2014 the same cosine similarity introduced on the jargon slide, now shown as a concrete number a retrieval system would use to decide whether a result is confident enough to trust.

Metrics

Why hybrid search wins

KeywordExact word match only
61%
VectorMeaning-based search
78%
HybridKeyword + vector + rerank
92%
These bars show recall@10 \u2014 of the documents that actually answer a given question, what fraction show up in the top 10 results \u2014 on a representative internal benchmark comparing three retrieval strategies. Keyword-only search (BM25 / Elasticsearch style) misses paraphrases and synonyms entirely, capping recall around the low 60s no matter how the index is tuned. Vector-only search recovers most of that gap because it matches meaning, but it can still miss exact identifiers \u2014 product codes, legal clause numbers, proper names \u2014 that keyword search would have caught trivially. Hybrid retrieval runs both searches in parallel, merges the candidate lists (often with reciprocal rank fusion, "RRF"), and then reranks the merged set with a heavier cross-encoder model before returning the top results. The combination captures precision from keyword matching and recall from semantic matching, which is why production RAG systems increasingly default to hybrid rather than picking one strategy. The numbers here are illustrative of the shape of the result \u2014 vector clearly beats keyword, hybrid clearly beats vector \u2014 not a claim about any specific dataset.

Two models of knowledge

What’s similar vs. how things connect

flowchart LR
  subgraph vec["Vector database — finds similarity"]
    Q1["Question: best pasta in Rome?"] --> S1[Search by meaning]
    S1 --> R1["Review: Incredible cacio e pepe near Pantheon"]
    S1 --> R2["Blog: Top trattorias in the eternal city"]
  end
  subgraph grf["Graph database — follows connections"]
    P1[Restaurant] -->|"located in"| C1[Rome]
    P1 -->|"serves"| D1[Pasta]
    P1 -->|"reviewed by"| U1[Local guide]
    U1 -->|"also reviewed"| P2[Another restaurant]
  end
Left side — the vector database takes a question, converts it to a meaning representation, and compares it against stored representations of millions of document passages. The output is a ranked list of the most semantically similar documents: the review mentioning "incredible cacio e pepe" and the blog about "top trattorias" both surface for "best pasta in Rome" because their meaning vectors are close to the query's vector. The database knows nothing about the relationships between those documents — it only measures similarity. Right side — the graph database stores the world as a network. Each entity becomes a node: the restaurant, the city, the dish, the local guide, another restaurant. Each relationship becomes a labelled edge: "located in", "serves", "reviewed by", "also reviewed." To answer a question about this restaurant, a graph traversal follows the edges outward from the restaurant node, collecting connected facts at each hop. The result isn't a list of similar documents — it's a structured map of related entities. Following "reviewed by" to the local guide, then "also reviewed" from that guide to another restaurant, answers "what other places did the same reviewer recommend?" — a question that cannot be answered by similarity search at all. The two databases answer fundamentally different questions and are best used together: vector search finds the entry points most relevant to a query; graph traversal expands those entry points with connected context.
03

Act 3

Maps of knowledge

"Maps of knowledge" is a precise description of what graph databases store. In a flat document library, knowledge is text on a page — unstructured, with relationships implied in the prose but not explicitly stored. In a graph database, those relationships are first-class citizens: every connection between two entities is a named, queryable edge. A knowledge map can answer questions that require traversing multiple relationships: "find all suppliers whose products are used in equipment governed by this regulation, and list the compliance contacts for each." No amount of document search answers that question — it requires following a chain of stored relationships. Graph databases are designed exactly for this kind of traversal.

When to use what

The right database for the right question

Vector database

“Find what’s similar to this.”

  • Document search & Q&A
  • Product & content recommendations
  • Image and voice similarity
  • AI chatbot knowledge base

Graph database

“Show me how these things connect.”

  • Fraud detection networks
  • Organisational knowledge maps
  • Multi-step research paths
  • Supply chain traceability

Many production systems use both

Vector search finds the entry points. Graph traversal follows the connections. Together they give AI the full picture.

Vector database — the defining question is "what is similar to this?" Document search and Q&A: a support agent asks a question in plain language; the system finds the ten most semantically relevant knowledge-base articles even if the exact words never appear. Product and content recommendations: a user reads an article; the system identifies other articles with similar meaning-space coordinates and surfaces them. Image and voice similarity: photographs and audio recordings are converted to meaning vectors just like text; a photo of a shoe finds other shoes without any metadata. AI chatbot knowledge base: the retrieval layer that gives a language model its up-to-date, traceable source material. Graph database — the defining question is "how are these things connected?" Fraud detection: each transaction, account, and device is a node; relationships between them are edges; an investigator traverses edges to find the full network behind a suspicious transfer. Organisational knowledge maps: people, teams, documents, and projects stored as nodes; "who worked on anything related to this product line" is a graph traversal. Multi-step research paths: starting from one scientific paper, following citation edges and author edges reaches a cluster of related work. Supply chain traceability: a defective component connects to supplier, batch, shipment, and retail destination via stored relationship edges. Why-panel — neither technology alone is sufficient for the hardest AI tasks. Vector search locates the right documents; graph traversal reveals the relationships between the entities inside those documents. A fraud investigation starts with "find transactions similar to known fraud patterns" (vector), then follows "who else is connected to this account" (graph) — each step answers a different kind of question.

Decide in 30 seconds

Vector or graph \u2014 which one?

\u2713
"What's similar to this?" \u2014 reach for a vector database.
\u2713
"How are these things connected?" \u2014 reach for a graph database.
\u2713
Need both \u2014 similarity to find entry points, then relationships to expand them \u2014 combine them in one pipeline.
\u2713
Still unsure? Start with vector \u2014 most retrieval questions turn out to be similarity questions.
This is the practical decision rule the whole deck has been building toward. If the question an application needs to answer is fundamentally about likeness \u2014 find documents like this one, find products like this one, find images like this one \u2014 a vector database is the right default: fast approximate nearest-neighbor search over embeddings, exactly as shown on the earlier code and terminal slides. If the question is fundamentally about traversal \u2014 who is connected to whom, which entities sit between two other entities, how many hops separate two nodes \u2014 a graph database is the right tool: fraud rings, organizational maps, and supply-chain traceability all live here, as covered on the compare slide. Many production RAG systems eventually need both: vector search narrows millions of documents down to a relevant handful, then a graph traversal enriches those results with the relationships between the entities they mention. Teams starting from zero should still pick one to start \u2014 defaulting to vector is the pragmatic choice, since retrieval questions ("what document answers this?") vastly outnumber traversal questions in most applications, and a graph layer can always be added later once a genuine multi-hop use case appears.
“
The best AI isn’t the one that knows the most — it’s the one that knows where to look.
Vectors find similarity · Graphs follow connections · Retrieval ties both into answers you can trust
"Where to look" is the operational constraint the quote points at. An AI model's knowledge is fixed at training time — it cannot absorb tomorrow's report, last week's regulation change, or the document your team wrote this morning. The model's reasoning capability is largely independent of its knowledge currency; a highly capable model fed stale or absent information still produces stale or absent answers. The architecture described across this presentation addresses exactly that gap: retrieval-augmented AI separates the model's reasoning layer from its knowledge layer. The citation beneath the quote names the three layers that work together. "Vectors find similarity" — meaning search converts any question into a coordinate and finds the nearest knowledge; language-independent, fast at scale, tolerant of paraphrase. "Graphs follow connections" — relationship traversal answers questions that require chaining through entities; the answer is assembled by following stored edges rather than by matching text. "Retrieval ties both into answers you can trust" — trust has two components: accuracy (the answer is grounded in retrieved documents, not invented) and traceability (the source document can be cited and verified). The three layers are not alternatives; they are complementary layers of the same architecture.
